title: Power BI Intro
content:
Power BI Introduction

Business Intelligence Redefined

Microsoft Power BI is a powerful business analytics service that provides interactive visualizations and business intelligence capabilities with an interface simple enough for end users to create their own reports and dashboards.

It allows you to connect to hundreds of data sources, simplify data prep, and drive ad hoc analysis. Produce beautiful reports, then publish them for your organization to consume on the web and across mobile devices.

title: Getting Started
content:
Getting Started with Power BI

Power BI Desktop

Most users start with Power BI Desktop, a free application you install on your local computer that lets you connect to, transform, and visualize your data.

To begin, download and install Power BI Desktop. When you open it, you'll see the Report View, where you build visualizations, the Data View, and the Model View for managing relationships.

title: Data Sources
content:
Connecting to Data

Importing Information

Power BI can connect to a wide variety of data sources, including Excel, SQL Server, Web, CSV, and many cloud-based services like Azure and Salesforce.

Common Sources
  • Excel Workbooks (.xlsx)
  • Text/CSV files
  • SQL Server Databases
  • Web Pages (scraping tables)
  • OData Feeds

Simply click Get Data on the Home ribbon to see the full list of supported connectors.

title: Power Query
content:
Transforming Data (Power Query)

Cleaning and Shaping

Before visualization, data often needs cleaning. Power Query Editor is the tool used for this. It records every step you take (like removing columns or filtering rows) as a script in the M language.

Common Transformations
  • Remove Rows: Get rid of duplicates or nulls.
  • Pivot/Unpivot: Reshape columns into rows or vice versa.
  • Split Columns: Break a 'Full Name' into 'First Name' and 'Last Name'.
  • Merge Queries: Combine data from multiple tables (like a SQL Join).

title: DAX Basics
content:
DAX Basics

Data Analysis Expressions

DAX is the formula language used in Power BI for creating custom calculations in Calculated Columns and Measures. It is similar to Excel but more powerful for relational data.

Simple DAX Measure
Total Sales = SUM(Sales[Amount])

Measures are dynamic calculations where the results change in response to user interaction in the report (like filtering by date).

title: Calculated Columns
content:
Calculated Columns vs Measures

Row-Level vs Aggregated

Calculated Columns are computed row by row and stored in the model. Use them when you need to use the value in a slicer or as a row/column header.

Calculated Column Example
Full Name = [FirstName] & " " & [LastName]

Measures are calculated on the fly during report interaction. They are generally better for performance when calculating totals or averages.

title: Visualizations
content:
Creating Visuals

Visualizing Trends

Building a report is as simple as dragging fields from the Fields pane onto the Report canvas. Power BI automatically picks a visual, but you can change it in the Visualizations pane.

  • Bar/Column Charts: For comparisons.
  • Line Charts: For trends over time.
  • Pie/Donut Charts: For proportional parts of a whole (use sparingly!).
  • Maps: For geographic data.
  • Cards: To highlight a single key metric (KPI).

title: Filters & Slicers
content:
Interactivity

Slicers and Filters

Reports become interactive through Slicers and Filters.


Slicers are on-canvas visuals that let users filter all other visuals on the page (e.g., filtering a sales chart by a specific 'Category' button). The Filters pane allows you to set filters on a specific visual, a whole page, or the entire report.

title: Model View
content:
Model View

Managing Data Structure

The Model View in Power BI Desktop allows you to see all the tables in your data model and how they are connected. This is where you define the architectural foundation of your report.

You can create diagrams, set properties for columns and measures, and organize fields into folders to keep your model clean and manageable.

title: Star Schema
content:
Data Modeling (Star Schema)

Fact and Dimension Tables

The Star Schema is the recommended data modeling approach in Power BI. It consists of two types of tables:

  • Fact Tables: Contain quantitative data (e.g., Sales Amount, Quantity) and foreign keys.
  • Dimension Tables: Contain descriptive attributes (e.g., Product Name, Customer Region, Date).

Organizing data this way improves performance and makes it easier for users to understand and navigate the data model.

title: Relationships
content:
Managing Relationships

Connecting the Dots

Relationships define how data flows between tables. The most common type is the One-to-Many (1:*) relationship (e.g., one Product can appear in many Sales records).

Power BI uses Cross-filter direction (Single or Both) to control how filtering applied to one table propagates to another. Keeping relationships simple and avoiding many-to-many links is crucial for accurate reporting.

title: Calendar Tables
content:
Calendar Tables

The Backbone of Time Analysis

A dedicated Calendar (Date) Table is essential for performing time-based calculations. It must contain one row for every day without gaps.

Creating a Table with DAX
Calendar = CALENDARAUTO()

Once created, you can add calculated columns for Year, Month, Quarter, and Weekday to enable flexible time filtering.

title: Advanced DAX - CALCULATE
content:
Advanced DAX (CALCULATE)

Changing Filter Context

The CALCULATE() function is the most important function in DAX. It evaluates an expression in a modified filter context.

CALCULATE Example
Red Product Sales = CALCULATE(
    SUM(Sales[Amount]), 
    Products[Color] = "Red"
)

CALCULATE allows you to perform complex comparisons, such as calculating a product's sales as a percentage of total parent category sales.

title: Time Intelligence
content:
Time Intelligence

Analyzing Trends Over Time

Power BI provides built-in functions for comparing data across different time periods. These are called Time Intelligence functions.

Year-to-Date Example
Sales YTD = TOTALYTD(SUM(Sales[Amount]), 'Date'[Date])

Common functions include SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR, DATEADD, and PARALLELPERIOD, which make it easy to build growth and trend reports.

title: Row-Level Security
content:
Row-Level Security (RLS)

Data Access Control

Row-Level Security (RLS) allows you to restrict data access for specific users based on their roles. This ensures that a Regional Manager only sees sales data for their own region.

You define roles and rules in Power BI Desktop using DAX filters (e.g., [Region] = "East") and then assign users to these roles in the Power BI Service.

title: Dashboards vs Reports
content:
Dashboards vs Reports

Knowing the Difference

In the Power BI Service, Reports and Dashboards serve different purposes:

  • Reports: Multi-page documents with detailed visuals and deep-dive interactivity.
  • Dashboards: Single-page summaries ('Canvas') that pin visuals ('Tiles') from one or more reports.

Dashboards are designed for high-level monitoring, while reports are for detailed exploration and analysis.

title: Publishing
content:
Sharing Your Work

Power BI Service

After creating your report in Power BI Desktop, you share it by Publishing it to the Power BI Service (the cloud-based version).


Once published, your colleagues can view and interact with the report in their web browsers or mobile apps without needing Power BI Desktop installed.

title: Final Summary
content:
Power BI Summary 🚀

Insights Architect! 🏆

Congratulations! You've learned the end-to-end workflow of Power BI: from connecting to data, transforming it with Power Query, modeling with Star Schemas, mastering DAX (including CALCULATE), and creating interactive visualizations.

Power BI is an essential skill for modern data analysts. Keep exploring advanced DAX functions and custom visuals to truly master your data storytelling!
